hook: You already know how to translate, rotate, and reflect shapes — and you've seen how transformations can move a figure around the plane without changing its size or shape. Now imagine tiling your bathroom floor: you need identical shapes that fit together perfectly, covering every square inch with no gaps and no overlaps. Which shapes can actually do this, and why do some shapes work while others leave awkward gaps? That's exactly the question tessellations force us to answer.
concept: A tessellation (or tiling) is an arrangement of one or more shapes that covers an entire plane with no gaps and no overlaps. Formally, a tessellation is a partition of the plane $\mathbb{R}^2$ into regions (tiles) such that every point belongs to exactly one tile or lies on a shared boundary. When only one type of regular polygon is used, we call it a regular tessellation.
anatomy: Every tessellation has three key structural elements worth naming precisely. First, the tile is the repeating polygon (or set of polygons) whose copies fill the plane — in a regular tessellation each tile is a regular $n$-gon with interior angle measure $\frac{(n-2)\cdot 180°}{n}$. Second, the vertex point is any point where the corners of multiple tiles meet; for the tiling to work with no gaps, the interior angles of all tiles meeting at a vertex must sum to exactly $360°$. Third, the transformation rule is the specific isometry — translation, rotation, or reflection — that maps one tile onto an adjacent tile, explaining how the pattern propagates across the plane.

worked_example.description_latex: We start with the formula for the interior angle of a regular polygon because the entire question of whether a shape can tessellate hinges on what happens when its corners meet at a vertex point. Recall that this formula comes from dividing any convex polygon into triangles — a polygon with $n$ sides splits into $(n-2)$ triangles, each contributing $180°$.

Let's test the equilateral triangle first. Each interior angle is $60°$, and we need to check whether some whole number of these angles can fit around a vertex point summing to exactly $360°$. Dividing $360° \div 60° = 6$, which is a whole number, so six equilateral triangles fit perfectly around every vertex — confirming that equilateral triangles tessellate.

Squares have interior angles of $90°$, and exactly four squares meet at each vertex to give $4 \times 90° = 360°$ — this is why square floor tiles work so well. The tessellation is generated by two perpendicular translations, shifting one tile horizontally and vertically by the side length.

Regular pentagons have interior angles of $108°$, and $360° \div 108° = 3.\overline{3}$, which is not a whole number. This means we cannot arrange a whole number of pentagons around any vertex without either leaving a gap or forcing an overlap, so regular pentagons cannot tessellate the plane on their own.

Hexagons have interior angles of $120°$, and exactly three fit around every vertex ($3 \times 120° = 360°$), producing the familiar honeycomb pattern. Together with equilateral triangles and squares, regular hexagons are the only three regular polygons that can tile the plane by themselves — these are the three regular tessellations.

partial_example.description_latex: We want to determine whether a regular octagon can form a regular tessellation by itself. Our strategy is always the same: compute the interior angle, then check whether $360°$ divided by that angle gives a whole number.
Here we apply the interior angle formula to find that each angle of a regular octagon measures $135°$. This is the key value we need before we can test the vertex condition.

Now divide $360°$ by the interior angle you just found. Is the result a whole number? Write out the division, state whether it is a whole number, and conclude whether regular octagons can tessellate the plane on their own.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: State the three regular polygons that can form a regular tessellation and give the interior angle of each.
Calculate the interior angle of a regular 12\text{-gon (dodecagon) and determine whether it can tessellate the plane by itself. Show your division.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \frac{(12-2)\cdot180°}{12} = 150°;\quad 360°\div150° = 2.4 \text{ (not a whole number) — does NOT tessellate}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: At a vertex in a tessellation, three regular polygons meet: two equilateral triangles and one unknown regular polygon. Find the number of sides $n$ of the unknown polygon.
practice_problems.answer_latex: 2(60°) + \frac{(n-2)\cdot180°}{n} = 360° \implies \frac{(n-2)\cdot180°}{n} = 240° \implies 180n - 360 = 240n \implies n = -6 \text{ (impossible); re-check: two triangles } + \text{ one hexagon: } 120°+120°+120°=360°,\; n=6

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A designer places a regular tessellation of equilateral triangles on a coordinate grid so that one triangle has vertices at $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, and $\left(\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$. Write the translation vector that maps this triangle to the adjacent triangle directly to its right.

A honeybee builds a honeycomb using regular hexagonal cells. Each cell has a side length of $3\text{ mm}$. Explain in one sentence why the hexagonal shape is one of only three regular polygon shapes that could theoretically tile the comb with no wasted space, and compute the interior angle that makes this possible.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Regular hexagons tessellate because their interior angle } 120° \text{ divides } 360° \text{ evenly (}3\times120°=360°\text{), one of only three regular polygons with this property.}

common_mistakes: Students often forget to divide $360°$ by the interior angle and instead try to divide $360°$ by the number of sides $n$ directly — this confuses the number of sides with the angle size, giving a meaningless result. Always compute the interior angle first using $\frac{(n-2)\cdot180°}{n}$, then divide $360°$ by that angle.

A frequent error is concluding that because a polygon cannot form a regular tessellation by itself, it cannot appear in any tessellation at all — but semi-regular tessellations combine two or more types of regular polygons, and non-regular polygons (like any triangle or quadrilateral) can always tessellate. Be careful to limit the 'does not tessellate' conclusion to regular tessellations with that single polygon.

Students sometimes check only that $360° \div (\text{interior angle})$ is close to a whole number and round, accepting values like $3.3$ or $2.9$ as 'close enough.' The vertex angle sum must be exactly $360°$ — any deviation, no matter how small, produces either a gap or an overlap over the infinite plane.
untested_variants: Semi-regular (Archimedean) tessellations, where two or more different regular polygons meet at every vertex in the same order — for example, the 3.3.3.3.6 tiling mixing triangles and hexagons.

Determining whether a general (non-regular) quadrilateral or triangle can tessellate the plane, using the argument that any triangle tessellates because two copies form a parallelogram, and any quadrilateral tessellates because its four angles sum to $360°$.
Tessellations generated primarily by reflections or glide reflections (rather than translations), including Escher-style artistic tilings where the tile is a non-polygonal interlocking shape.

Problems involving the interior angle sum condition at a vertex when more than three distinct polygon types meet, requiring students to set up and solve a multi-term equation such as $\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3 + \theta_4 = 360°$.
